annotations.label: Scalar multiplication as vector space operation

anchor.snippet: scalar multiplication is one of the basic operations defining a vector space

annotations.note: Mathlib's `Module` typeclass is the scalar multiplication structure used for vector spaces by taking scalar type a field and vector type an additive group.

annotations.label: Scalar multiplication as module operation

anchor.snippet: a module in abstract algebra

annotations.note: `Module` directly formalizes scalar multiplication of a semiring on an additive commutative monoid as the module operation.

annotations.label: Positive real scalar preserves Euclidean vector direction

anchor.snippet: multiplies the magnitude of the vector without changing its direction

annotations.note: `SameRay.sameRay_pos_smul_right` says a positive scalar multiple stays in the same ray, with `norm_smul_of_nonneg` giving the corresponding norm scaling in real normed spaces.

annotations.label: Scalar multiplication product type

anchor.snippet: Scalar multiplication is the multiplication of a vector by a scalar

annotations.note: `Module` provides the scalar multiplication operation `r • x : M`, generalizing scalar-times-vector multiplication beyond vector spaces.

annotations.label: Inner product distinction

anchor.snippet: inner product of two vectors

annotations.note: Mathlib defines inner products separately via `Inner`, but I found no declaration whose statement is the explanatory distinction from scalar multiplication.

annotations.label: Scalar multiplication function

anchor.snippet: scalar multiplication is a function from K × V to V

annotations.note: `Module` represents this operation as the curried scalar action `r • x : M`, equivalent to a function on scalar-vector pairs.

annotations.label: Scalar multiplication notation

anchor.snippet: The result of applying this function to k in K and v in V is denoted k v

annotations.note: Mathlib uses the `SMul` notation `k • v` for scalar multiplication rather than the article's juxtaposition `k v`.

annotations.label: Scalar additivity

anchor.snippet: Additivity in the scalar

annotations.note: `add_smul` states `(r + s) • x = r • x + s • x` for modules.

annotations.label: Vector additivity

anchor.snippet: Additivity in the vector

annotations.note: `smul_add` states `a • (x + y) = a • x + a • y` for distributive scalar actions.

annotations.label: Compatibility with scalar product

anchor.snippet: Compatibility of product of scalars with scalar multiplication

annotations.note: `mul_smul` is the action axiom `(a * b) • x = a • b • x`.

annotations.label: One scalar identity

anchor.snippet: Multiplying by 1 does not change a vector

annotations.note: `one_smul` states `(1 : R) • x = x` for a scalar action by a monoid.

annotations.label: Zero scalar gives zero vector

anchor.snippet: Multiplying by 0 gives the zero vector

annotations.note: `zero_smul` states `(0 : R) • x = 0` for scalar actions with zero.

annotations.label: Negative one gives additive inverse

anchor.snippet: Multiplying by −1 gives the additive inverse

annotations.note: `neg_one_smul` states `(-1 : R) • x = -x` for modules over rings.

annotations.label: Plus sign convention

anchor.snippet: Here, + is addition either in the field or in the vector space

annotations.note: The overloaded meanings of `+` are handled by Lean typeclasses and appear in `add_smul`, but the convention itself is not a separate theorem.

annotations.label: Zero convention

anchor.snippet: 0 is the additive identity in either

annotations.note: Mathlib has typeclass-polymorphic zeros and `zero_smul` for scalar zero acting on a vector, but the notation convention is not a standalone declaration.

annotations.label: Juxtaposition convention

anchor.snippet: Juxtaposition indicates either scalar multiplication or the multiplication operation in the field

annotations.note: Mathlib keeps scalar action `•` distinct from multiplication `*`, with `smul_eq_mul` only identifying them for the regular self-action.

annotations.label: Coordinate space interpretation

anchor.snippet: The space of vectors may be considered a coordinate space

annotations.note: Mathlib models coordinate spaces as function types with `Pi.module`, but the broad interpretive sentence is not itself a formal statement.

annotations.label: Field units form group

anchor.snippet: The units of the field form a group K ×

annotations.note: `Units.instGroup` states that the units of any monoid, hence of a field's multiplicative monoid, form a group.

annotations.label: Scalar multiplication as group action

anchor.snippet: scalar-vector multiplication is a group action on the coordinate space by K ×

annotations.note: `Units.instDistribMulAction` gives units a distributive scalar action whenever the underlying monoid acts, covering nonzero field scalars acting on modules such as coordinate spaces.

annotations.label: Zero scalar collapses coordinate space

anchor.snippet: The zero of the field acts on the coordinate space to collapse it to the zero vector

annotations.note: `zero_smul` says scalar zero sends every vector to the zero vector in any compatible scalar action.

annotations.label: Real scalar multiplication scales length

anchor.snippet: it stretches or contracts vectors by a constant factor

annotations.note: `norm_smul_of_nonneg` states that multiplying a vector in a real normed space by a nonnegative scalar scales its norm by that scalar.

annotations.label: Real scalar multiplication preserves or reverses direction

anchor.snippet: it produces a vector in the same or opposite direction of the original vector but of a different length

annotations.note: `sameRay_neg_smul_right_iff_of_ne`, together with the positive-scalar ray lemmas, formalizes that nonzero real scalar multiples lie in the same ray or the ray of the negated vector according to the sign.

annotations.label: Field as vector space over itself

anchor.snippet: V may be taken to be K itself

annotations.note: `Semiring.toModule` makes any semiring a module over itself, specializing to a field as a vector space over itself.

annotations.label: Scalar multiplication in field case

anchor.snippet: scalar multiplication may then be taken to be simply the multiplication in the field

annotations.note: `smul_eq_mul` identifies the scalar action of a multiplicative type on itself with its multiplication operation.

annotations.label: Coordinate vector space

anchor.snippet: When V is K n

annotations.note: `Pi.module` gives coordinate function spaces `ι → K` the expected module structure, generalizing finite `K^n`.

annotations.label: Componentwise scalar multiplication

anchor.snippet: scalar multiplication is equivalent to multiplication of each component with the scalar

annotations.note: `Pi.smul_apply` states that scalar multiplication on a function space is evaluated componentwise.

annotations.label: Module scalar multiplication over commutative ring

anchor.snippet: The same idea applies if K is a commutative ring and V is a module over K

annotations.note: `Module` is defined over a `Semiring`, so it includes modules over commutative rings as a special case.

annotations.label: Scalar multiplication over a rig

anchor.snippet: K can even be a rig

annotations.note: Mathlib's `Module` uses `Semiring` scalars, which is the usual typeclass for rigs.

annotations.label: Rig has no additive inverse

anchor.snippet: then there is no additive inverse

annotations.note: Mathlib's `Semiring` does not require additive inverses while `Ring` adds additive group structure, but there is no universal theorem that semirings have no additive inverses.

annotations.label: Left and right scalar multiplication

anchor.snippet: the distinct operations left scalar multiplication c v and right scalar multiplication v c may be defined

annotations.note: Mathlib models right scalar multiplication using the opposite type, with `op_smul_eq_mul` identifying `MulOpposite.op a • b` with `b * a`.

annotations.label: Left scalar multiplication of a matrix

anchor.snippet: The left scalar multiplication of a matrix A with a scalar λ gives another matrix of the same size as A

annotations.note: `Matrix.smul_apply` defines scalar multiplication on matrices entrywise, and the result has the same `Matrix m n` type.

annotations.label: Left scalar multiplication notation

anchor.snippet: It is denoted by λ A

annotations.note: Mathlib has the operation formalized, but its notation is `λ • A` rather than juxtaposed `λ A`.

annotations.label: Left scalar multiplication entries

anchor.snippet: whose entries of λ A are defined by

annotations.note: `Matrix.smul_apply` states `(r • A) i j = r • A i j`, the entrywise definition of left matrix scalar multiplication.

annotations.label: Right scalar multiplication of a matrix

anchor.snippet: the right scalar multiplication of a matrix A with a scalar λ could be defined to be

annotations.note: `Matrix.op_smul_eq_mul_diagonal` models right scalar multiplication as an opposite scalar action equal to multiplying by the scalar diagonal matrix on the right.

annotations.label: Left and right matrix scalar multiplication agree over commutative field

anchor.snippet: these two multiplications are the same

annotations.note: `Matrix.smul_eq_mul_diagonal` proves the right-diagonal form agrees with scalar multiplication over any commutative semiring, hence over a commutative field.

annotations.label: Matrix scalar multiplication over commutative fields

anchor.snippet: can be simply called scalar multiplication

annotations.note: Mathlib formalizes the equality that justifies using one scalar multiplication over commutative scalars, but the naming convention is not a declaration.

annotations.label: Left and right matrix scalar multiplication may differ over noncommutative fields

anchor.snippet: they may not be equal

annotations.note: `Matrix.scalar_comm_iff` relates equality of left and right scalar forms to commutation with matrix entries, but I found no explicit noncommutative-field counterexample declaration.

annotations.label: Real scalar and matrix example

anchor.snippet: For a real scalar and matrix

annotations.note: `Matrix.smul_apply` provides the entrywise mechanism for real scalar matrix computations, but I found no declaration formalizing the article's specific numerical example.

annotations.label: Quaternion scalar and matrix example

anchor.snippet: For quaternion scalars and matrices

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `Quaternion` and matrix scalar multiplication infrastructure exists, but I found no declaration formalizing the article's concrete quaternion matrix example.

annotations.label: Quaternion units

anchor.snippet: where i , j , k are the quaternion units

annotations.note: `QuaternionAlgebra.basisOneIJK` provides the one/i/j/k coordinate basis, but I found no separate named unit constants matching the prose exactly.

annotations.label: Quaternion noncommutativity blocks equality of left and right products

anchor.snippet: The non-commutativity of quaternion multiplication prevents the transition of changing ij = + k to ji = − k

annotations.note: Quaternion multiplication component formulas such as `Quaternion.imK_mul` support deriving the ij versus ji behavior, but I found no theorem stating this matrix-scalar obstruction directly.
